---
title: "Section 14: Two-Sample Mean Inference"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, error = TRUE)
```

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
```

## CI for a difference in means

### Data tidying

`cat_breeds_clean.csv` contains data on characteristics of domestic cats of different breeds.

```{r}
cats <- read_csv("../../data/cat_breeds_clean.csv")
```


### Data exploration

Let's compare the basic summaries for each group (fixed vs intact male ragdoll cats).

```{r}
cats_twomeans_summary <- cats %>% 
  filter(Age_in_years >= 1) %>%
  filter(Sex == "male", Breed == "Ragdoll") %>% 
  group_by(Fixed) %>% 
  summarize(avgTime = mean(Sleep_time_hours),
            sdTime = sd(Sleep_time_hours),
            n = n())

cats_twomeans_summary
```

Let's also extract the values and save them for later.

```{r}
xbar1 <- cats_twomeans_summary$avgTime[1]
xbar2 <- cats_twomeans_summary$avgTime[2]
s1 <- cats_twomeans_summary$sdTime[1]
s2 <- cats_twomeans_summary$sdTime[2]
n1 <- cats_twomeans_summary$n[1]
n2 <- cats_twomeans_summary$n[2]
```


## Equal variances T

Assuming equal variances, what is the pooled standard deviation?

```{r}
s_p <- sqrt(((n1 - 1)*s1^2 + (n2 - 1)*s2^2) / (n1 + n2 - 2))

s_p
```

Now, we can make a 90% confidence interval for the difference in means.

```{r}
pt_est <- xbar1 - xbar2

se <- s_p * sqrt(1/n1 + 1/n2)

cv <- qt(0.95, df = n1 + n2 - 2)

c(pt_est - cv*se, pt_est + cv*se)
```

We can also perform a hypothesis test.

```{r}
test_stat <- (xbar1 - xbar2 - 0) / se
test_stat
```

The p-value is calculated on the T with n1 + n2 - 2 degrees of freedom.

```{r}
2*pt(-0.737459, df = n1 + n2 - 2)
```

We can check our result with `t.test()`.

```{r}
df1 <- cats %>%
  filter(Age_in_years >= 1) %>%
  filter(Sex == "male", Breed == "Ragdoll", Fixed == F) 

df2 <- cats %>%
  filter(Age_in_years >= 1) %>%
  filter(Sex == "male", Breed == "Ragdoll", Fixed == T)

# Notice we have to set var.equal = T
t.test(df1$Sleep_time_hours, df2$Sleep_time_hours,
       mu = 0, conf.level = 0.9, var.equal = T)
```



## Unequal variances T 

What if we don't want to assume equal variances?  Let's compare intact male cats and kittens.

```{r}
cats <- cats %>%
  mutate(Age_category = case_when(
    Age_in_years < 1 ~ "kitten",
    Age_in_years >= 1 ~ "adult"
  ))

cats_age_summary <- cats %>% 
  filter(Sex == "male", Fixed == F) %>% 
  group_by(Age_category) %>% 
  summarize(avgTime = mean(Sleep_time_hours),
            sdTime = sd(Sleep_time_hours),
            n = n())

cats_age_summary
```

Even though the standard deviations are similar, we can still decline to make the equal variance assumption.  The unequal variances (Welch) test is more general.

```{r}
xbar1 <- cats_age_summary$avgTime[1]
xbar2 <- cats_age_summary$avgTime[2]
s1 <- cats_age_summary$sdTime[1]
s2 <- cats_age_summary$sdTime[2]
n1 <- cats_age_summary$n[1]
n2 <- cats_age_summary$n[2]
```

Let's calculate the approximate df for comparing the difference in means.

```{r}
w_numer <- (s1^2/n1 + s2^2/n2)^2
w_denom <- (s1^4/(n1^2*(n1-1)) + s2^4/(n2^2*(n2-1)))

w <- w_numer / w_denom
```

Now, we can build a CI.

```{r}
pt_est <- xbar1 - xbar2
se <- sqrt(s1^2/n1 + s2^2/n2)

cv <- qt(0.975, df = w)

c(pt_est - cv*se, pt_est + cv*se)
```

Now, confirm with `t.test`.

```{r}
df1 <- cats %>%
  filter(Sex == "male", Fixed == F, Age_category == "adult")

df2 <- cats %>%
  filter(Sex == "male", Fixed == F, Age_category == "kitten")

# We don't need to set var.equal since FALSE is the default
t.test(df1$Sleep_time_hours, df2$Sleep_time_hours,
       conf.level = 0.95, var.equal = F)
```

## H-test for a difference in means

Are adult cats more sleepy than kittens on average?  In other words, is $\mu_A - \mu_K$ greater than 0?

```{r}
# Summary statistics from before
xbar1 <- cats_age_summary$avgTime[1]
xbar2 <- cats_age_summary$avgTime[2]
s1 <- cats_age_summary$sdTime[1]
s2 <- cats_age_summary$sdTime[2]
n1 <- cats_age_summary$n[1]
n2 <- cats_age_summary$n[2]

# Calculate the test statistic and p-value here!

test_stat <- (xbar1 - xbar2) / sqrt(s1^2/n1 + s2^2/n2)

test_stat

pt(test_stat, df = w, lower.tail = F)


```


## Paired T test

We want to test whether the difference (growth) in height from age 13 to 14 is positive.

```{r}
thirteen <- c(44.1, 59.0, 65.9, 58.7, 49.3)
fourteen <- c(46.3, 60.5, 68.2, 59.4, 50.6)

# This works the way we want it to
growth <- fourteen - thirteen
```

```{r}
n <- length(growth)
# Note: n is quite small here, we only have 5 individuals; so using the t-distribution instead of the normal is important!

xbar <- mean(growth)
s <- sd(growth)

test_stat <- (xbar - 0)/(s/sqrt(n))

test_stat
```

We have an observed test statistic of 5.39, and a null T with 4 df. The p-value is the area above our test statistic.

```{r}
pt(test_stat, df = n-1, lower.tail = F)
```


### Check in R

```{r}
t.test(growth, alternative = "greater")

t.test(fourteen, thirteen, paired = T, alternative = "greater")
```

If we don't pair tthe data, we get a non-significant result.

```{r}
t.test(fourteen, thirteen, alternative = "greater")
```
